data.title: Why freezing rain has millions at risk of losing power — and heat
data.description:
ATLANTA (AP) — Every morning this week, Newberry Electric Cooperative CEO Keith Avery walks into his office and turns on The Weather Channel. Then he starts making calls, lining up crews and equipment to respond to outages if a forecasted ice storm cripples power across South Carolina.

Avery has dealt with disasters before. Nearly every one of his 14,000 customers lost power when the remnants of Hurricane Helene tore through in 2024.

But the approaching ice storm has him even more worried because ice-coated trees and power lines can keep falling long after the storm itself has passed.

“I hate ice storms,” Avery said. “They are worse than hurricanes.”

Officials across the eastern two-thirds of the U.S. have been sounding the alarm about the potential for freezing rain to wreak havoc on power systems. In the South, especially, losing electricity doesn’t just mean the lights going out. It means losing heat.

That’s because a majority of homes are heated by electricity in Alabama, Arkansas, Georgia, Kentucky, Louisiana, Mississippi, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas and Virginia, according to the U.S. Census Bureau.

Ice storms, Avery said, are especially punishing because of what happens after they move out: Crews struggle to reach damaged lines on ice-covered roads; cold, wet weather takes a toll on workers; and problems can linger for days as ice-laden branches continue to snap.

“You get a power line back up and energized, and just as you leave, you hear a loud crack and boom, there’s a tree limb crashing through what you just repaired,” Avery said.

Lessons from Winter Storm Uri

Texas experienced the worst-case scenario in 2021, when Winter Storm Uri’s freezing temperatures crippled the state’s power grid for five days and led to 246 storm-related deaths, according to the Texas Department of Health Services.

But experts say Uri’s damage stemmed largely from poorly weatherized power plants and natural gas systems, not downed power lines.

“The main lesson was to enforce requirements for utilities to be ready for cold weather,” said Georg Rute, CEO of Gridraven, a Texas-based firm that analyzes power system risks for grid operators.

Rute said utilities have applied lessons from Uri, and while he does not expect a repeat of that kind of grid collapse, he warned that other vulnerabilities remain, including transmission lines tripping during extreme cold.

Gov. Greg Abbott on Thursday gave assurances to Texans about the state’s power grid. The Electric Reliability Council of Texas has said grid conditions are expected to be normal during this weekend’s storm.

“The ERCOT grid has never been stronger, never been more prepared, and is fully capable of handling this winter storm,” Abbott said.

The governor added, though, that residents could lose power as ice weighs down power lines and trees fall onto them. But, he said, energy providers are prepositioned to fix any outages, and there’s been an effort to clear trees and branches near power lines in recent years.

Outages hit hardest in vulnerable communities

Winter Storm Uri also exposed disparities in how outages affected communities, said Jennifer Laird, a sociology professor at the City University of New York’s Lehman College who studies energy insecurity. Researchers have found that residents in predominantly Hispanic areas experienced more outages, while Black residents were more likely to face outages lasting a day or more.

Laird said outages expose vulnerabilities people don’t anticipate, from medical equipment that requires electricity to families with infants who rely on refrigeration for breast milk. Younger households and those with lower levels of education, in particular, are less likely to have contingency plans in place, she said.

“There are lots of ways that we’re dependent on energy that we don’t realize until a crisis hits — and then it really exposes those vulnerabilities,” Laird said.

Even if this weekend’s storm does not produce significant outages, the financial burden on families could linger for months. About 1 in 6 U.S. households are already behind on their energy bills, and with millions expected to turn up their heaters during the cold snap, that number could rise, Laird said.

“A month or two after the storm hits, suddenly the bill hits,” she said. “We could see a rise in disconnection notices and disconnections.”

Utilities prepare for the worst

Utilities in the Southeast have also warned customers to prepare for possible outages. Duke Energy, which serves more than 4.6 million customers in North and South Carolina, urged residents to be ready for multiple days without power. The utility said more than 18,000 workers would be ready to respond once conditions are safe.

The Tennessee Valley Authority, which serves more than 10 million people across seven states, said it has invested hundreds of millions of dollars in weatherization since a 2022 winter storm and has built-in redundancies to reroute power if a line goes down.

“It takes a lot of snow and ice to down one of those big lines,” TVA spokesperson Scott Brooks said.

data.title: Baby teething toy sold on Amazon recalled after 30+ choking incidents
data.description:
(WJW) — A popular baby teething toy sold on Amazon is being recalled after more than 30 choking incidents have been reported, according to the U.S. Consumer Product Safety Commission.

The recall is for nearly 7,000 Yetonamr Pull String Teething Toys, which were sold on Amazon from June to October 2025. Consumers should stop using the product immediately.

According to the recall alert, CPSC is aware of 32 choking incidents. CPSC says the recalled toys “violate the mandatory standard for toys” because the silicone strings are smaller than permitted.

“The silicone strings can reach the back of the throat and become lodged, posing a risk of respiratory distress, serious choking hazard and death,” states the recall alert.

The teething toy is described as:

  • Off-white disc shape with either a red ball or blue ball in the middle and six long, silicone pull string “tentacles” of various colors running through the ball.
  • There are also three free-spinning rings and soft push buttons on the disc.
  • “Model no. 688-59” printed on the product packaging

Parents and caregivers are advised to “immediately” stop using the toy and contact Longyanguiheng to receive a full refund. For more details on the teething toy recall visit CPSC.gov.

data.title: Glenn Youngkin is the Republican to watch if Vance and MAGA falter
data.description:
In the Trump era, every day brings a theater-of-the-absurd mini-drama. Monday’s headline, “Trump links Greenland threats to missing out on Nobel Prize,” looked like an AI spoof, but it wasn’t.

President Trump’s foreign policy is based on whether the actions of allies or enemies are personally pleasing or displeasing to him. And he acts like a conquering king but then backs down. Meanwhile, there is widespread agreement that undoing or weakening the 80-year post-World War II NATO alliance would be a gift to Russia.

What a week of absurdity it was! On Tuesday, Trump posted an AI-generated map on Truth Social, set in the Oval Office and surrounded by European leaders. The map showed the U.S., Canada, Greenland and Venezuela covered in America’s stars and stripes, as if they were one nation. Trump is visually flaunting his desire to expand U.S. territory, with a “dare to stop me” attitude.

Call it “diplomacy for narcissists.” Federal Republican officeholders and candidates on the November ballot must either support or disavow this new American aggression, which also applies domestically. For a vast swath of Americans, the November midterms and the 2028 presidential election can’t come soon enough.

Now, entering from stage right is Virginia’s newly minted former governor, Glenn Youngkin. You may not know his name, but expect to hear and see more of him.

Before Youngkin won his first public office in November 2021 by less than 2 points, he had served as co-chair of the Carlyle Group, a prestigious investment firm. During the campaign, Youngkin was known for deliberately keeping his distance from then-former President Trump. Nonetheless, the ever-humble Trump took credit for Youngkin’s win.

Over the ensuing four years, Youngkin was a popular governor with steady job approval around 54 percent. But since Virginia governors are limited to one four-year term, at last Saturday’s inauguration, he became an unemployed multimillionaire. (More on that later.)

Youngkin is a moderate in an increasingly far-right party. He is not considered MAGA-crazy and is respected in elite leadership and monied circles. The former governor is well-spoken and has a record of economic success in Virginia. He is often labeled a contender for the 2028 Republican presidential nomination and seen as having a presidential aura. Moreover, Youngkin could attract moderates from both parties and independents, all eager to move past Trump’s drama with a new face.

All are reasons why Youngkin’s recent “Fox News Sunday” announcement surprised political pundits, when he told host Jacqui Heinrich, “I agree with President Trump, I agree with Marco Rubio: I think Vice President Vance would be a great nominee.”

Immediately, speculation shifted to whether Youngkin is running for Vance’s potential vice presidency, angling for a high-profile position in Trump’s Cabinet, or both. Youngkin’s way-too-early “endorsement” also positions him for a possible VP fight with Secretary of State Rubio.

However, the prospects of a “Vance coronation” were criticized by Trump’s first-term press secretary, Fox News host Kayleigh McEnany. She challenged Vance’s heir-apparent status, as The Hill reported in its headline: “Kayleigh McEnany urges GOP to seek competitive primary in 2028: ‘It makes you tougher.’”

Ironically, Youngkin would be well-suited to “toughen” Vance. Although Youngkin has ruled himself out for now, a change of heart is possible. Vance could falter — perhaps in part because he will be forced to defend Trump amid a backlash against him, even as Trump hovers above him and demands loyalty.

The final chapter of Youngkin’s gubernatorial reign dangerously pitted him against MAGA. The “voice of MAGA,” Steve Bannon, hosts a daily War Room podcast. This powerful platform makes him a kingpin in the movement, with his own agenda.

Immediately following Virginia Republicans’ devastating defeat in November’s election, Bannon posted on X: “Glenn Youngkin, you just ended your political career last night. You destroyed the Republican Party in Virginia for a GENERATION. You pushed a Never-Trumper candidate and made it so she was the only option! Absolute disaster.”

Youngkin’s high job approval ratings failed to carry over to the Republican gubernatorial candidate, Lt. Gov. Winsome Earle-Sears (R), whom Youngkin endorsed but Trump did not. The hard truth was that a massive anti-Trump wave propelled the Democrats’ gubernatorial candidate, moderate former Rep. Abigail Spanberger (D), to an unexpected 15.5-point victory. She is now Virginia’s first female governor.

In context, Trump lost Virginia by only 5.7 percentage points in 2024, but in 2020, he lost by 10 points. What infuriated Bannon was that there was no Republican gubernatorial primary, where Bannon believed Youngkin could have pushed for a more Trump-friendly nominee.

Besides winning the historic governor’s mansion, Virginia Democrats swept the lieutenant governor and attorney general offices. Additionally, Democrats increased their 51-to-49 majority in the House of Delegates to a whopping 64–36 advantage ahead of the statehouse session, where redistricting by November’s midterm elections is an action item.

Interestingly, during Youngkin’s pivotal Fox News interview, he refused to acknowledge that anti-Trump sentiment drove Virginia’s Republican wipeout. Obviously it did, but Spanberger’s message focused on affordability. Shortly after, Trump learned that “affordability” was resonating with voters, so he mocked the word.

Curious about Youngkin’s 2028 prospects, I asked Mark Halperin, the famous editor in chief of the live interactive video platform 2WAY and host of Next Up. He answered: “Governor Youngkin is poised to be a strong presidential candidate for the post-Trump Republican Party — outsider credentials, money, quick with a smart elbow — but there is no way to know for sure if Youngkin is tough enough, agile enough, and national enough to win both a nomination fight and a general election contest.”

All true, but we do know that November’s midterm elections will also be a “beauty contest” featuring Vance, Rubio and Youngkin, along with other prospective 2028 Republican leaders. They will campaign nationwide, arguing that Republicans must retain control of Congress while defending Trump’s policies, using pretzel logic for most.

Then, in 2028, Trump will attempt to dominate the Republican primaries and the general election like no president in U.S. history, aggressively shaping his legacy.

Myra Adams is a political and religious opinion writer who served on the creative team of two Republican presidential campaigns in 2004 and 2008.

data.title: Thune comes under GOP pressure to bypass Democrats on health care
data.description:
Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) is coming under increasing pressure from Senate Republican colleagues to bring health care legislation to the Senate floor amid fears of a Democratic wave in November.

Conservatives are pushing the GOP to move a package similar to President Trump’s new health care reform proposal through the budget reconciliation process, which would allow it to avoid a Democratic filibuster and pass the Senate with a simple majority.

Thune has so far resisted that maneuver, though he says using the special budget reconciliation process to circumvent Democratic resistance is an “option.”

He is also getting pressure from moderate and swing-state Republicans to vote on a package to extend enhanced Affordable Care Act (ACA) health insurance subsidies that expired this month.

Republicans such as Sens. Susan Collins (Maine), Josh Hawley (Mo.) and Bernie Moreno (Ohio) want to extend the subsidies and make reforms to the program.

But the bipartisan talks to extend and reform the enhanced ACA tax credits have hit a brick wall, and senators in both parties are broadly skeptical of reaching a deal.

This is giving conservatives led by Sen. Rick Scott (R-Fla.) new political momentum to push Thune to pass an all-Republican health care reform plan by either using the budget reconciliation process or interpreting the Senate’s rules in new ways to make it tougher for Democrats to block it.

“Whether we do it through reconciliation or we do a standing filibuster, whatever we do, let’s fix health care. If the Democrats want to vote against it, let them vote against it. Let’s actually do something to fix it,” Scott told The Hill in a phone interview.

Scott, who has extensive experience in health care management as the former CEO of Columbia/HCA Healthcare, has introduced a bill that he says largely mirrors Trump’s new health care agenda.

The Florida senator, who challenged Thune for the Senate majority leader’s job after the 2024 election, has strong ties to Trump-allied Senate conservatives who are chafing over Democrats’ ability to block the president’s legislative agenda since the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act in July.

Republican senators are getting antsy about passing health care legislation to address soaring health insurance premium costs.

KFF, a nonpartisan health research organization, projects that more than 22 million Americans who receive subsidies through the ACA insurance marketplaces will see their annual premium payments rise from an average of $888 in 2025 to $1,904 in 2026

Scott argues that under the Senate’s existing rules, Thune could require Senate Democrats to actively hold the floor with continuous debate, possibly lasting throughout the evening hours, to prevent Republicans from advancing a health care reform package.

In recent decades, a senator has only had to announce an objection on the Senate floor to stop a bill in its tracks and require a 60-vote threshold to allow it to advance.

“Those are rules that we don’t have to change anything, we have to go do it,” Scott said.

He says that health care talks with Democrats, which have dragged on since the 43-day government shutdown in the fall, are headed toward a dead end.

“All they’re talking about doing is having government pay for something rather than fix the system. Health care costs too much because nobody’s a shopper,” he said. “Give people an account, make them be shoppers.”

Scott’s bill wouldn’t revive the enhanced premium health insurance tax credits that expired this year, but it would allow states to convert the value of the premium tax credits that were set up by the original Affordable Care Act into contributions to “Trump Health Freedom Accounts,” according to an analysis by KFF.  

“If you look at where the president is, it’s basically my bill with a few add-ons,” Scott said.

Trump’s plan would not extend the ACA tax credits that expired earlier this month and would instead redirect insurance subsidies to health savings accounts, which individuals and families could use to buy health care.

Trump’s plan would also require insurance companies to report how frequently they deny care while calling for a crackdown on pharmacy benefit managers, the companies that occupy the space between pharmaceutical manufacturers and consumers. Many Republicans blame them for rising prescription drug costs.  

Trump has also called on Congress to pass legislation to codify his most-favored-nation drug-pricing deals to give Americans access to the same low prices people in other countries pay for prescription drugs.

Other Republicans, including Sen. John Kennedy (La.), are pressing Thune to use the budget reconciliation process to circumvent Democratic objections to free market-based GOP health care reforms.

But Kennedy said Thune and other GOP leaders are reluctant to use the same legislative fast-track to pass health care reform that they used last year to enact sweeping tax reforms.

“I’ve concluded we just have a difference of opinion. For whatever reason John is reluctant to do reconciliation. … I think he will look back and go, ‘We made a mistake not doing it.’”

Kennedy speculated Thune is reluctant to attempt to pass sweeping health care reforms with only Republican votes because of deep divisions within the party over how to reform ObamaCare or whether to repeal it entirely.

The effort to repeal the law during Trump’s first term failed spectacularly when the late Sen. John McCain (R-Ariz.) voted against it on the Senate floor, joining Collins, Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) and every Senate Democrat in voting “no.”

“The only [reason] I can see not to do it is that we might fail. That to me is not a good reason,” Kennedy said.

The Louisiana Republican warned the issue of rising health care costs, along with the issue of affordability writ large, threatens to wipe out Republicans in the 2026 midterm election.

“I’m an optimist who worries and I try to be realistic about the world. Cost of living will be the issue in the midterms,” Kennedy said.

He observed that “for a variety of reasons, many people are blaming us” for high health care costs, “and I think we have to address that.”

“The only way I know how to do it is through reconciliation,” he said. “I think we will look back if we don’t do it and ask ourselves, ‘What in God’s name were we thinking?’”

Thune hasn’t ruled out using budget reconciliation to advance a Republican-sponsored health care reform plan, but he has yet to embrace that option.

Thune told reporters before the Martin Luther King Jr. holiday that House Republicans are looking at moving a reconciliation package later this year.

“We got people over here who want to do it as well, who want to use that legislative vehicle again, but we have to sort of figure out — you don’t just put a reconciliation bill on the floor, you actually have to have a specific reason you want to do it,” he said.

He said the president and senior White House officials would have to be heavily “engaged” in pushing a new reconciliation package for it to have any chance of moving through the Senate.

data.title: Ruben Gallego, a bright spot in a bad year for Democrats, hears White House calls
data.description:
The 2024 election was a dark chapter for Democrats nearly across the board, as they lost not only the presidential race but also both the House and the Senate. 

Sen. Ruben Gallego’s win in Arizona, against that backdrop, was one of the few bright spots of the cycle for the party. 

In winning his Senate seat in a competitive red state, Gallego figured out a way to appeal not only to Hispanic voters — who ended up largely supporting President Trump — but also cutting through to the working class, a vital demographic seeping away from the Democratic Party.  

Now, some of the senator’s allies argue Gallego could apply the same strategy in a 2028 presidential election. 

Sure, they acknowledge, it will be a crowded field with the likes of California Gov. Gavin Newsom, Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker and Pete Buttigieg, the former Transportation secretary, expected to enter the race.

But they say Gallego should not be discounted.

“He’s won in a red state and overperformed with demographics that you need to win a presidential campaign, not only with Latino voters but male voters, suburban voters, and some Republican voters,” said Democratic strategist Chuck Rocha, who serves as an adviser to Gallego.

“Yes, there were a few other Democratic senatorial campaign wins in states that Kamala Harris lost, but only in one of those instances did a Senate candidate win where there’s more registered Republicans than there are Democrats.” 

For the skeptics who say Gallego, 46, is too young and has only served in the Senate a short time, Rocha offered this retort: “I would refer you to one Barack Obama.” 

“At a moment when the Achilles heel for the Democratic Party is Latinos and working class voters, this is his opportunity to rescue our country,” he said. 

While he has advisers like Rocha in his ear, Gallego is still deciding whether or not to jump in the race, allies say. He has told reporters he has three young children at home and is focused on his current job in the Senate. But he’s also appearing in all the places someone testing the waters for a run would go. In February, for example, he is heading to Nevada, where he is expected to headline the state party’s inaugural “Fighting the Good Fight” event. 

And those who know him well say the senator, who also served five terms in the House, would do well if he entered. 

“If he wants it, there’s a path for him,” Rebecca Katz, the prominent Democratic consultant who advises Gallego, told The Hill. “And I say that as someone to the left of him.” 

Democratic strategists and donors who see a path argue his background would appeal to voters across the country. 

In an election that will likely revolve around the economy, Gallego, who grew up working class and the son of a single mom, is someone who understands what it’s like to live paycheck to paycheck. 

“Growing up poor, I remember how desperate things were for me,” the senator told Stephen Colbert on his CBS late night show in October.

“And I know people in my neighborhoods in Arizona were feeling very desperate. Things were really hard, right? And I think people were trying to say it wasn’t hard. But people couldn’t buy homes. They couldn’t … fully go to the grocery store. I saw people who weren’t filling up their gas tanks and then we were all lying to ourselves [saying] ‘the economy is getting better,’ and I realized I couldn’t lie to them because I was that person at one point in my life.” 

“At one point in your life when things are really bad, you just want someone to give you hope,” he told Colbert. “And I wanted to be that person. … That was the best campaign to run at that point.”

The secret to Gallego’s success, Democrats say, is that he is the epitome of the American dream. While he grew up working class, he ended up at Harvard. He also served in the Marines and is bilingual. 

During his Senate race in 2024, Gallego hosted viewing parties for boxing fights, appeared at rodeos, and visited construction sites where he handed out burritos and coffee. 

In a survey out this week conducted by Noble Predictive Insights, a Phoenix-based research firm, Gallego emerged as the No. 1 politician in the state. 

“If Democrats are serious about broadening their coalition in 2028, Gallego checks a lot of boxes,” said Mike Noble, the pollster who conducted the survey, who added that Gallego’s appeal to a wide swath of voters and his “authentic” communication style “is a powerful mix in a national environment where trust and relatability matter more than ideology alone.” 

In the survey, first reported this week by KTAR News, Gallego outperformed Sen. Mark Kelly (Ariz.), who has also grown in popularity and could be considering jumping into the 2028 Democratic primary. 

“The senator has surged in popularity,” Matt Grodsky, a Democratic strategist based in Arizona, said of Gallego. “While he has made clear he is focused on his job as senator, it says a lot about the importance of Arizona as a battleground that our two senators are in that conversation.”

Brian Seitchik, a Republican strategist in the state, said Gallego won’t perform well if he runs for president. He argued Gallego is too liberal to win a presidential race.

“It will not take long for voters to figure out who he is: a truly progressive, liberal lunatic,” Seitchik said. “He was able to mask that reality in the campaign last year but that will not be the case should he run for president.” 

Still, in recent months, Gallego has been a key surrogate in races important to Democrats including the gubernatorial elections in New Jersey and Virginia and the mayoral race in Miami. All three races were won by Democrats. 

“He resonates with a broader electorate because he understands that costs are too high and he knows how to speak to voters in an authentic way,” said Democratic strategist Xochitl Hinojosa. “That’s why Donald Trump was elected and why you have politicians like Barack Obama succeed, and Ruben provides that to voters.” 

And with immigration enforcement in the headlines, Hinojosa said Gallego also knows how to talk to voters about that complex issue “in a humane way.”  

“He doesn’t have to build that trust in a way other Democrats have,” she said. 

While Gallego hasn’t made a decision about the 2028 race, he has taken the steps of a potential candidate, making the rounds on an early buzz-fueled circuit last summer. 

In August, for example, he spoke at a “Politics & Eggs” event at Saint Anselm College in New Hampshire, a must-stop for anyone considering wading into primary waters. 

Shortly before that event, he also appeared at a town hall in Iowa where he ripped President Trump, saying Americans were “now sicker and poorer” because of his policies. 

Rocha said Gallego would succeed because “he’s not Pete Buttigieg” and “he’s not as slick as the California governor.” 

“They’re all going to have their moment in the sun but they’re all going to crash down,” he said. “They’re all good Dems, but their profiles don’t set them up for what America is demanding right now.” 

Gallego, Rocha said, “came from literally nothing.” 

“That’s the kind of person you need to win in 2028,” he said.

data.title: Voice of America's Persian service blacks out Iran's exiled crown prince
data.description:
The Persian service for President Trump’s overhauled Voice of America (VOA) is censoring coverage of Iran’s exiled Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi, who played a central role in stirring up antigovernment protests that rocked the country earlier this month. 

Three staff members at VOA told The Hill that the adviser overseeing VOA’s Persian-language service, Ali Javanmardi, is excluding any video or audio of protesters chanting Pahlavi’s name, silencing guests from discussing Pahlavi and skewing Trump’s position toward the crown prince. 

“This is the kind of censorship that you would expect to see in authoritarian countries run by dictatorships,” said one of the staffers who spoke to The Hill.

Javanmardi and U.S. Agency for Global Media head Kari Lake, who oversees VOA Persian, have issued public statements defending their decision to avoid coverage of Pahlavi, arguing it’s not their role to promote certain Iranian opposition figures. 

“I am neither a spokesman for the opposition nor its enemy,” Javanmardi wrote in a post Jan. 18 on the social platform X, responding to a growing online campaign accusing him of directing the censorship at VOA and promoting separatist views. “I only stand by the truth, the truth that is written every day in the streets of Iran with blood and courage. My commitment is neither to figures nor to flags, but to the people of Iran, who silently but steadfastly fight for freedom and human dignity.”

However, the VOA sources who spoke to The Hill paint a different picture. Speaking on the condition of anonymity out of fear of legal, personal and professional retaliation, they detailed what they described as an unprecedented violation of journalistic ethics and distortion of facts at the taxpayer-funded broadcaster. 

They accuse Javanmardi, who is Iranian Kurdish, of pushing forward his personal political bias against Pahlavi, the son of the last shah of Iran, who has long lived in the United States. The crown prince is putting himself forward as an interim leader for the country if the Islamic Republic is overthrown, calling for protesters to take to the streets as he works to boost his support in Washington, D.C.

“Ali Javanmardi is a longtime, lifetime opposition to Reza Pahlavi, accused of being a Kurdish separatist. It shows that, at least implicates that, there [is a] personal agenda, as well,” the staffer said. 

Javanmardi holds the title of special contractor or senior adviser overseeing Persian, Kurdish, and Afghan television for Voice of America. One source said the title has no precedent at VOA, calling it “made up.” 

Javanmardi’s activities have drawn the scrutiny of the National Union for Democracy in Iran (NUFDI), an Iranian American, nonpartisan, nonprofit organization that backs Pahlavi. The group has presented a formal complaint to congressional lawmakers on Javanmardi’s “unprofessional conduct.” 

“In multiple instances, he has spoken explicitly against Iran as a national concept and political entity, rather than distinguishing between the regime and the country or its people,” the group wrote in its complaint. 

“His biased rhetoric is evident in Persian-language broadcasts through inflammatory and ideologically driven framing. In Kurdish-language content, he has explicitly characterized Iran as the primary enemy of the Kurdish people, a position that constitutes political advocacy rather than journalism.”

NUFDI also cited Javanmardi’s social media posts and YouTube videos making incorrect claims that the Iranian army had defected in 2022, his false claims that Pahlavi was cooperating with the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps — listed as a terrorist organization by the U.S. — and his criticism of Pahlavi as a “fascist.”

Javanmardi initially directed VOA staff not to cover a meeting between Pahlavi and Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) earlier this month, a source told The Hill, although a story about Graham’s meeting was eventually published. 

One VOA staff member said there was no coverage of Pahlavi’s widely publicized press conference in Washington last week. 

“By default, we know he wouldn’t let us cover it,” another VOA staff member said.  

And in its report on Trump’s recent interview with Reuters, VOA Persian covered the president expressing doubt over whether Pahlavi would be accepted by Iranians to run the country, but it excluded Trump saying, “certainly if they would, that would be fine with me.” By comparison, BBC Persian covered Trump’s remarks in full.

While Pahlavi’s calls for Iranians to return to the streets this past weekend seem to have gone largely unheeded, coming in the wake of a violent crackdown by state actors, he remains among the country’s highest-profile opposition figures.  

“Pahlavi reflects a direct foil to the Islamic Republic: A connection to a pre-revolutionary period of economic development and social freedom, and the prospect for a break from the regime’s failures at home and abroad,” Naysan Rafati, senior Iran analyst with the International Crisis Group, said in a statement to The Hill. 

“Though the extent of his support on the ground in Iran is difficult to gauge, it certainly seems to resonate with many of the protesters, and puts him in pole position among the opponents of the current regime.”

Trump has been noncommittal when asked directly whether he views Pahlavi as an alternative leader of Iran. But his special envoy for peace missions, Steve Witkoff, reportedly met with Pahlavi in early January. 

Lake did not respond to specific questions regarding claims made by VOA staffers and other critics of Javanmardi. 

“We are not in the business of selecting Iran’s political leadership,” Lake said in a statement to The Hill. “That would be irresponsible of any outside news organization. That decision belongs solely to Iran’s 93 million citizens, who have a right to self-determination. Right now, the story should be about them, not the numerous opposition groups outside Iran.” 

“Elevating external opposition figures over voices inside the country risks externalizing the conflict in ways that would only benefit the cruel regime and undermine protesters on the ground. It would be inappropriate to elevate a singular voice and as President Trump said, we should ‘see who emerges’ as the next leader of Iran,” she added. 

In some cases, VOA Persian has featured opposition voices living outside Iran. 

Javanmardi told staff on Jan. 15 they would not cover what they initially thought was a meeting between Iranian opposition figure Masih Alinejad and Sen. Dan Sullivan (R-Alaska) the previous day, citing VOA policy, two staffers said. (Alinejad did not meet with Sullivan last week, but the senator did post a message of support for the protesters in Iran alongside a photo with Alinejad from a 2023 meeting in his office.) 

But only two days earlier, VOA Persian had aired an extended interview with Alinejad, a longtime activist for a secular and democratic Iran who is not in the Pahlavi camp. Alinejad has not put herself forward as a political leader.

The allegations of censorship at VOA Persian extend to expert voices invited on the programming, who are told they cannot mention Pahlavi. 

Sahar Tahvili, a European-based expert on internet censorship, said she was reprimanded for mentioning Pahlavi’s name at the beginning of her interview with VOA Persian on Jan. 9 and was told that the outlet is not discussing the prince and any role he is playing in the protest.

Tahvili said she started her interview with VOA saying she wanted to send a message to the protesters in Iran, and those “answering the call from Reza Pahlavi, and they are making the country free soon.”

Tahvili said a producer criticized her as doing an advertisement for Pahlavi and said “‘you’re not allowed to mention his name.’”

The producer later sent a message to Tahvili that VOA policy is not to elevate any opposition figure and noted it is also excluding coverage of the Mojahedin-e-Khalq, a controversial opposition group once labeled a terrorist organization by the United States.

Tahvili said she was threatened with never being invited back on the program, where she has appeared since 2019. 

Originally from Iran, Tahvili said she grew up with VOA Persian, which for a time was the only Persian-language satellite news channel available to counter the propaganda from the Islamic Republic.  

“For us, in that dictatorship environment that we grew up, it was like the voice from the West,” she said. “People could speak freely, and different voices, and diversity of the guests, and diversity of the news, and covering as much as they could. It was very popular and it was the only news channel that we had at that time. It’s something in my heart. It’s painful to see how far VOA has drifted from that mission.”

Trump initially shut down all VOA services at the start of his second term. He revived a slimmed-down version of VOA Persian over the summer as a tool to disseminate America’s position to Iranians — as the U.S. and Iran traded strikes. 

Sources said Javanmardi was brought in at the beginning of recent protests in Iran, which began on Dec. 28 and have intensified over nearly three weeks. Analysts say they posed one of the greatest threats to the Islamic Republic’s survival. 

An unnamed Iranian official told Reuters earlier this week at least 5,000 protesters were killed throughout the protests, but human rights groups believe that number is far higher. Trump threatened military intervention on behalf of protesters but backed down after he said he received assurances that the Islamic Republic would not “hang” protesters. 

In Washington, D.C., Iranian opposition figures are jockeying for U.S. support for their vision of a new leadership in Tehran. 

Pahlavi is a divisive figure, criticized as being an Iranian outsider with an international network of support but little backing inside the country. His father, an autocratic ruler who used oppression and violence to keep power, was overthrown in 1979 during the Islamic Revolution. 

But despite these criticisms, Pahlavi has emerged as a central figure in the protests in Iran, with demonstrators regularly chanting his name and holding up images of his face. 

Sources alleged that Pahlavi’s blacklisting at VOA Persian is largely related to Javanmardi’s political bias, also noting that he avoids sending orders through email.

NUFDI, the pro-Pahlavi, Iranian American organization, in its complaint to lawmakers said Javanmardi has a “documented pattern of disinformation, bias, and ethical violations.”

The complaint cites Javanmardi’s social media posts during the 2022 protests — dubbed the Women, Life, Freedom protests in the wake of the killing of Mahsa Amini — where he claimed that Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was dead.

“The claims were posted repeatedly over several days and were not supported by credible evidence,” NUFDI wrote. 

“The false reports echoed a long-used disinformation tactic that generated unfounded hope, and disrupted the circulation of verified news, and contributed to confusion at a critical moment in the movement, ultimately undermining its cohesion and momentum that directly endangered protesters and undermined collective action.” 

At that time, Javanmardi posted as the founder and news director of AvaToday.net, which promoted itself as a news network to provide “factual news and commentary covering Iranian and US events for the Iranian people.”

One of the VOA staffers took particular issue with Lake reposting an X account that promotes ethnic minority rule of Iran. The account, “Turkmen sahra,” featured an interview Javanmardi gave on VOA Persian. 

“We know Kari Lake doesn’t know anything about Iran, and Ali suggests her to do these things to execute his personal agenda and attitude there, under the name of Kari Lake,” another VOA staffer said.  

Javanmardi, in his Jan. 18 post on X, said he supports a unified Iran and alleged being the target of a false narrative campaign. 

“The attacks against me today are not simply a disagreement or political criticism but the result of false narratives the Islamic Republic and its network of supporters — from organized bots to entities affiliated with the opposition — have created to destroy the truth and silence voices,” he wrote. 

“In my new job at VOA, my primary mission is to ensure it aligns with the US foreign policy and conveys [President Trump’s] support for the people of Iran,” he added.

data.title: Trump slams Newsom over Davos actions, possible White House ambitions
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday knocked California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) — a potential White House contender for 2028 — just a day after calling the governor a “good guy” in remarks at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

“Gavin Newscum, as a “Lame Duck” Governor of a Failing State, should not be at Davos running around screaming for the attention of Foreign Leaders, and embarrassing our Country. He made a mockery of himself, and everybody, including his staff, knows it,” the president wrote in a Truth Social post.

“With a record like he’s got, the ruination of one of the most beautiful places on Earth, where people are leaving in droves, it is unimaginable that he could run for President but, who knows, it’s a very strange World!” he added.

Newsom attended the Davos forum and spoke out against the Trump administration in comments to the press. He urged U.S. allies to “stand united” and “stand tall” in their fight against unprecedented White House foreign policy measures.

Newsom’s actions have prompted rebukes from administration officials — and even some in his own party — on the global stage.

On Tuesday, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent likened Newsom to notable pop culture figures like “American Psycho” character Patrick Bateman and a Ken doll.

“Gov. Newsom, who strikes me as Patrick Bateman meets Sparkle Beach Ken, may be the only Californian who knows less about economics than Kamala Harris,” the secretary said in remarks at the forum.

Newsom later responded and threw barbs of his own at Bessent.

“The Treasury secretary talked about a Barbie doll. It was as if he was reading a diary, and had just broken up with someone. I mean, it was a secretary of the Treasury using valuable time, yesterday, on the world stage,” Newsom said.

Since the start of his second term, Trump has accused Newsom of mismanaging the Golden State. The two sparred over the handling of California’s destructive wildfires last year.

data.title: Trump takes shot at NATO, says US should have invoked Article 5 'test' for border
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday said the United States should have put NATO to the “test” when it comes to the U.S. southern border by invoking Article 5, in which the multilateral organization states an armed attack against one member is considered an attack against all, triggering a unified response.

“Maybe we should have put NATO to the test: Invoked Article 5, and forced NATO to come here and protect our Southern Border from further Invasions of Illegal Immigrants, thus freeing up large numbers of Border Patrol Agents for other tasks,” the president wrote in a Truth Social post.

The remarks follow Trump’s Wednesday comments alleging the alliance might not fully stand behind the U.S. after receiving criticism from European Union leaders over his push to acquire Greenland.

“All we’re asking for is to get Greenland, including right, title and ownership because you need the ownership to defend it, you can’t defend it on a lease,” Trump said while attending the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

“The problem with NATO is we’d be there for them 100 percent but I’m not sure they’d be there for us,” the president added.

Hours after the comment, Trump told reporters he’d reached the first step in creating “framework” for a deal with Greenland involving NATO’s support for U.S. access to mineral rights in the self-autonomous Danish territory.

“This solution, if consummated, will be a great one for the United States of America, and all NATO Nations,” Trump wrote on Truth Social after meeting with NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte.

Early Thursday, Rutte lauded the deal, noting its approval from officials in Denmark who are “completely open” to an increased U.S. footprint, though he said a stepped-up U.S. presence was not directly discussed.

“When it comes to the protection of the Arctic, with a priority on Greenland, we have to spend more energy, more time, more focus on this because we know the sea lanes are opening up,” he said in an interview with Reuters.

“We will come together in NATO with our senior commanders to work out what is necessary,” the alliance chief said.

“I have no doubt we can do this quite fast,” Rutte added. “Certainly, I would hope for 2026, I hope even early in 2026.”

data.title: Paris Hilton goes to Washington: Advocacy the 'most meaningful work of my life'
data.description:
Paris Hilton says her advocacy efforts at the Capitol have been the “most meaningful work” of her life, but she is remaining tight-lipped on whether she’d jump into the political ring herself.

“Coming here to the Capitol, to D.C., and doing my advocacy work — it’s truly been the most meaningful work of my life. And I’m so proud to stand with this cause with these incredible women who are so strong,” Hilton told ITK on Thursday, when asked if she would ever consider launching a bid for public office. 

“I’m just happy to give support, and I love that I can come and shine my spotlight on so many causes that are in need of it,” the 44-year-old multi-hyphenate media personality said.

Hilton’s remarks came as she stood alongside Reps. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) and Laurel Lee (R-Fla.) as they championed the Disrupt Explicit Forged Images and Non-Consensual Edits (DEFIANCE) Act.

The legislation would allow victims of nonconsensual deepfake pornography to sue individuals who produce and distribute the AI-generated content. The legislation’s supporters urged Speaker Johnson to put the bill on the House floor for a vote after it passed by unanimous consent in the Senate last week.

“When I was 19 years old, a private, intimate video of me was shared with the world without my consent. People called it a scandal. It wasn’t — it was abuse,” Hilton said at a news conference outside the Capitol.

“There were no laws at the time to protect me. There weren’t even words for what had been done to me. The internet was still new, and so was the cruelty that came with it,” she said.

“Too many women are afraid to exist online, or sometimes to exist at all. And I know how that feels because I’ve lived it,” Hilton said.

“The DEFIANCE Act is a bipartisan bill that gives survivors a path to justice,” Hilton said during sometimes-emotional remarks. She was later overheard saying she sported sunglasses because she was teary-eyed during the event. 

Hilton has been a familiar sight on Capitol Hill in recent years. In 2024, she helped advocate for the Stop Institutional Child Abuse Act, a youth welfare measure. That bill was signed into law by then-President Biden in 2024.

That year, Hilton said she could potentially see herself running for office.

“After coming here so many times over the past couple of years and seeing that my voice really can make a difference, and I can really shine a spotlight on things that are important around the world to make it a better place and safer for children, I can maybe see that happening,” the author and entrepreneur said at the time.

Lee, one of the co-sponsors of the DEFIANCE Act, praised Hilton’s efforts and ability to build consensus and collaboration. So would Lee support Hilton adding “representative” to her resume?

“I am always thrilled when we have smart, talented, capable women running for the House of Representatives,” Lee said as Ocasio-Cortez grinned and nodded.

“A number of them are here with us today, and so I would be thrilled to add another one to our ranks,” Lee said. 

Hilton was eyed being swarmed by press and fans following the conference, before her husband, Carter Reum, seemingly doubled as a wrangler and guided her through the throng.

Before she exited to another area of the Capitol, Hilton held up a DEFIANCE Act sign to deliver a message to a camera.

“Legislation’s hot,” she said, in a nod to her signature catchphrase, “That’s hot.”

data.title: Trump rails against Powell: 'Fed has been hurt and discredited'
data.description:
President Trump railed against Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell — again — on Thursday for not dropping interest rates.

“Mortgage Rates just hit a Three Year Low despite Jerome ‘Too Late”’ Powell, and his never ending quest to keep Interest Rates high (against ‘TRUMP!’),” the president wrote on Truth Social.

“It just shows that he has been wrong all along, because the Market is overtaking his obstinance. The Fed has been hurt and discredited during Too Late’s reign!” he added.

Powell has frequently faced Trump’s ire over the last few months and was recently informed of a federal probe tied to his congressional testimony last year.

The Fed chair has said the investigation, which focuses on the ongoing renovation of the Federal Reserve’s headquarters in Washington, D.C., is really “about whether the Fed will be able to continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions—or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.”

“I have deep respect for the rule of law and for accountability in our democracy,” he said in addressing the investigation’s launch. “No one — certainly not the chair of the Federal Reserve — is above the law. But this unprecedented action should be seen in the broader context of the administration’s threats and ongoing pressure.”

Trump has been touting economic gains and job growth in addition to lauding the impact of his sweeping tariffs. The president has repeatedly urged Powell to acknowledge the success of his policies with lower interest rates.

In December, Trump threatened to sue Powell for “gross incompetence” in managing the bank’s renovations.

“We’re thinking about bringing a suit against Powell for incompetence. Because think of it, these aren’t outstanding buildings. These are small buildings,” Trump said during a Dec. 29 press conference alongside Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

The Fed cut rates by 0.25 percentage points at each of its last three meetings, with the interest rate most recently sitting between 3.50 and 3.75 percent.
